keynotes.title: Investigation of Cathode Electrolyte Interphase Formation via Coupling Liquid Electrochemical TEM and GC/MS

keynote.title: Investigation of Cathode Electrolyte Interphase Formation via Coupling Liquid Electrochemical TEM and GC/MS
keynote.core_problem: Understanding the formation and dissolution mechanisms of the cathode electrolyte interphase (CEI) to elucidate battery degradation.
contributions: First demonstration of coupled liquid electrochemical TEM (ec-TEM) and GC/MS to monitor CEI evolution in a realistic electrochemical environment.
Identification of dispersed CEI particles (not continuous film) under high-voltage cycling (4–6 V vs Li).
Discovery of a 36 nm amorphous CEI film without crystalline LiF formed at 2.5–5.5 V, linked to EC oxidation and HF-mediated LiF formation.
Proposal of a two-step LiF dissolution pathway involving electrolyte oxidation to soluble intermediates, followed by reduction at lower potential yielding LiF-dissolving species.
methods: Liquid electrochemical transmission electron microscopy (ec-TEM)
Gas chromatography/mass spectrometry (GC/MS)
Electrochemical cycling with in situ and ex situ analysis
Correlation of electrochemical response (e.g., anodic current peaks), gas/liquid phase composition, and nanoscale electrode surface observations
experiments_or_evidence: Cycling between 4 and 6 V vs Li: observed dispersed particles (not continuous film); identified LiF and amorphous phase via ec-TEM and compositional analysis.
Cycling between 2.5 and 5.5 V vs Li: detected anodic current peak; measured 36 nm amorphous thin film lacking crystalline LiF; attributed to EC oxidation → HF → LiF.
LiF dissolution experiments inferred from electrochemical signatures and post-cycling GC/MS/ec-TEM correlations suggesting two-step redox-mediated process.
assumptions: Observed CEI morphologies and compositions under liquid ec-TEM conditions are representative of bulk cell behavior.
GC/MS detection of gaseous/liquid species reflects interfacial reaction pathways relevant to CEI formation/dissolution.
The amorphous phase observed prevents dissolution at high potential — inferred from stability under cycling, not directly proven.
limitations: Findings limited to carbonate-based electrolytes (EC-containing).
Experiments conducted within specific voltage windows (4–6 V and 2.5–5.5 V); generalizability across other windows unconfirmed.
LiF dissolution mechanism is inferred from correlated electrochemical and analytical data, not directly visualized or kinetically resolved.
Liquid ec-TEM involves simplified model systems (e.g., single WE geometry, thin liquid layer) that may not fully replicate commercial cell interfaces.

gap_signals.text: Experiments conducted within specific voltage windows (4–6 V and 2.5–5.5 V); generalizability across other windows unconfirmed.
Findings limited to carbonate-based electrolytes (EC-containing).
LiF dissolution mechanism is inferred from correlated electrochemical and analytical data, not directly visualized or kinetically resolved.
Liquid ec-TEM involves simplified model systems (e.g., single WE geometry, thin liquid layer) that may not fully replicate commercial cell interfaces.
Findings require validation across different potential windows and with non-carbonate electrolytes to validate these findings.

important_claims.claim: Cycling between 4 and 6 V vs Li leads to dispersed particles instead of a continuous CEI film, partly composed of LiF and an amorphous phase that prevents dissolution at high potential.
important_claims.evidence: Direct observation via liquid ec-TEM combined with compositional inference from GC/MS and electrochemical response.

important_claims.claim: Cycling between 2.5 and 5.5 V vs Li forms a 36 nm amorphous thin film without crystalline LiF, attributed to EC oxidation producing HF and subsequent LiF formation at higher potential.
important_claims.evidence: Anodic current peak during cycling correlated with ec-TEM observation of amorphous film and GC/MS detection of HF-related species.

important_claims.claim: LiF dissolution follows a two-step pathway: electrolyte oxidation forms soluble intermediates, later reduced at lower potential to yield species capable of dissolving LiF.
important_claims.evidence: Correlation of electrochemical transients, post-cycling gas/liquid analysis (GC/MS), and CEI morphology changes observed in ec-TEM.

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: High: Provides a validated multimodal methodology (ec-TEM + GC/MS + electrochemistry) for mechanistic CEI studies; reveals voltage-dependent CEI morphology/composition transitions and testable dissolution hypotheses; highlights critical gaps requiring cross-electrolyte and cross-potential validation.

keynotes.title: Mechanisms of Enhanced Electrochemical Performance by Chemical Short-Range Disorder in Lithium Oxide Cathodes

keynote.title: Mechanisms of Enhanced Electrochemical Performance by Chemical Short-Range Disorder in Lithium Oxide Cathodes
keynote.core_problem: Structural degradation and low reversible capacity (∼140 mAh/g) in LiCoO2 cathodes due to destructive phase transitions at high voltages (>4.2 V vs Li/Li+).
contributions: Identifies suppression of hexagonal-to-monoclinic transition at Li0.5CoO2 and O3-to-H1-3 transition at Li0.25CoO2 via CSRD.
Reveals mechanistic origins of enhanced phase stability: reduced c-axis lattice variation, increased oxygen vacancy and dimer formation energies, stabilization of Co in Li layers.
Explains high Li+ diffusivity via low-barrier 0-TM diffusion channels and expansion of diffusion network from 2D to quasi-3D.
Demonstrates band gap narrowing and enhanced electronic conductivity due to Co valence changes and linear Li-O-Li configurations.
Extends findings to Li-rich Li1.2Co0.8O2, suggesting improved capacity and cycling stability.
methods: Ab initio molecular dynamics (AIMD) simulations
Machine learning-accelerated AIMD
experiments_or_evidence: Suppression of hexagonal → monoclinic phase transition at Li0.5CoO2
Suppression of O3 → H1-3 phase transition at Li0.25CoO2
Reduced c-axis lattice variation
Increased oxygen vacancy formation energies
Increased oxygen dimer formation energies
Stabilization of Co atoms in Li layers during delithiation
High Li+ diffusion coefficients attributed to low-barrier 0-TM channels and quasi-3D diffusion network
Band gap narrowing and enhanced electronic conductivity linked to Co valence state changes and linear Li-O-Li configurations

gap_signals.text: No experimental validation reported; relies solely on theoretical (ML-accelerated AIMD) results.
Limited to LiCoO2 and Li1.2Co0.8O2; no evaluation across broader class of layered oxides or other disorder types.

important_claims.claim: CSRD suppresses hexagonal-to-monoclinic phase transition at Li0.5CoO2.
important_claims.evidence: Ab initio molecular dynamics simulations accelerated by machine learning.

important_claims.claim: CSRD suppresses O3-to-H1-3 phase transition at Li0.25CoO2.
important_claims.evidence: Ab initio molecular dynamics simulations accelerated by machine learning.

important_claims.claim: CSRD enhances Li+ diffusion via low-barrier 0-TM channels and quasi-3D diffusion network.
important_claims.evidence: Simulation-derived Li+ diffusion coefficients and structural analysis of diffusion pathways.

important_claims.claim: CSRD narrows band gap and enhances electronic conductivity via Co valence changes and linear Li-O-Li configurations.
important_claims.evidence: Electronic structure analysis from ab initio simulations.

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: Provides atomistic design principles for leveraging chemical short-range disorder to stabilize layered oxide cathodes; methodology (ML-accelerated AIMD) is transferable to other disordered battery materials.

keynotes.title: Impact of Innovation and Project Control on Sustainable Project Success: Examining the Mediating Role of Project Governance in Pakistan’s Software Development Sector

keynote.title: Impact of Innovation and Project Control on Sustainable Project Success: Examining the Mediating Role of Project Governance in Pakistan’s Software Development Sector
keynote.core_problem: How innovation and project control jointly influence sustainable project success, and whether project governance mediates this relationship—particularly in Pakistan's software development sector.
contributions: Empirically validates the mediating role of project governance between innovation, project control, and sustainable project success.
Provides context-specific evidence from a developing economy (Pakistan) in the software development industry.
Offers practical guidance for balancing innovation and formal project control to enhance long-term project viability.
methods: Quantitative research design.
Structured questionnaire using a five-point Likert scale.
Data collection from 300 valid responses by project managers, team members, and stakeholders.
Structural Equation Modelling (SEM) analysis in STATA 18.
experiments_or_evidence: SEM results confirm significant positive direct effects of innovation and project control on sustainable project success.
SEM results support full or partial mediation of project governance in the innovation → sustainable success and project control → sustainable success pathways.
Findings indicate higher likelihood of sustainable project outcomes when innovation capacity is aligned with governance and project control systems.
assumptions: Project governance is a measurable, distinct construct that meaningfully mediates between innovation/control and sustainability.
Sustainable project success can be reliably captured via self-reported survey items in the Pakistani software context.
Responses from project managers, team members, and stakeholders collectively represent valid organizational-level constructs.
limitations: Geographically bounded to Pakistan’s software development firms—limits generalizability to other sectors or economies.
Cross-sectional design prevents causal inference about temporal dynamics of mediation.
Reliance on self-reported data may introduce common method bias.

gap_signals.text: Cross-sectional design prevents causal inference about temporal dynamics of mediation.
Geographically bounded to Pakistan’s software development firms—limits generalizability to other sectors or economies.
Reliance on self-reported data may introduce common method bias.
Limited integration of project governance as a mediator between innovation and control in sustainability models.
Empirical evidence within developing economies is scarce in project management literature on sustainability.
Few studies apply SEM to test complex mediation pathways linking innovation, control, governance, and sustainability in software projects.

Five-point Likert scale instrument measuring innovation, project control, project governance, and sustainable project success.

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: Provides a validated, context-grounded measurement model and mediation framework for studying sustainability in global south software project settings; reusable for replication, extension to other sectors, or comparative cross-country studies.

keynotes.title: Promoting Reversible Anionic Redox in Sodium-Ion Cathodes by Doping and Phase Control

keynote.title: Promoting Reversible Anionic Redox in Sodium-Ion Cathodes by Doping and Phase Control
keynote.core_problem: Traditional sodium-ion cathode materials are limited by transition-metal-only redox, restricting capacity and high-voltage operation; reversible anionic (oxygen) redox remains poorly understood and unpredictably controllable.
contributions: Identified bond valence mismatch as a key descriptor correlating with reversible oxygen redox activation in layered Na-ion cathodes.
Discovered five dopants (K, Cu, Rb, Cs, Tl) that induce high bond valence mismatch and promote reversible oxygen redox when doped at 10% into Na0.66MnO2.
Demonstrated critical phase-dependent behavior: P2 polymorph enables reversible oxygen redox, while P'2 leads to irreversible oxygen redox.
Revealed atomic-scale mechanisms—large dopants increase O–O separation (suppressing O2 evolution/dimerization) and suppress detrimental TM migration (e.g., Cu stabilizes Mn lattice position).
Provided predictive design principles for stable anionic redox via dopant selection and crystal phase control.
methods: High-throughput computational screening of >50 dopants across the periodic table.
Synthesis of doped Na0.66MnO2 in both P2 and P'2 layered polymorphs.
Electrochemical testing (voltage profiles, cycling, overpotential analysis).
Synchrotron-based X-ray Absorption Spectroscopy (XAS).
Wavelet Transform Extended X-ray Absorption Fine Structure (WT-EXAFS).
Resonant Inelastic X-ray Scattering (RIXS).
experiments_or_evidence: Electrochemical testing showing reversible oxygen redox at ~4.2 V and ~4.5 V vs. Na/Na+ in doped P2 materials, but irreversibility in P'2 analogues.
RIXS confirming electron-hole localization on oxygen (not transition metals) in doped P2 at high voltage.
WT-EXAFS evidence of suppressed Mn migration and Cu-mediated stabilization of Mn positions in P2.
XAS and WT-EXAFS showing dopant-induced local structural distortions and increased O–O separation.
Correlation between computed bond valence mismatch and observed oxygen redox reversibility across dopants.
assumptions: Bond valence mismatch is a valid and predictive electronic-structural descriptor for oxygen redox activity.
P2 and P'2 polymorphs are structurally isolable and representative of distinct oxygen coordination environments governing redox reversibility.
Synchrotron spectroscopies (RIXS, WT-EXAFS) can unambiguously distinguish oxygen-centered redox from transition-metal redox and quantify local cation migration.
limitations: Limited to Na0.66MnO2 host framework; generalizability to other Mn-free or multi-TM hosts not demonstrated.
Dopant level fixed at 10%; optimal doping concentration per dopant not systematically varied.
Long-term cycling stability (>100 cycles) and full-cell performance not reported.
No direct quantification of oxygen redox capacity contribution vs. TM redox (e.g., via differential capacity or titration).
Computational screening details (e.g., DFT functional, oxidation state treatment) not specified in provided text.

gap_signals.text: Computational screening details (e.g., DFT functional, oxidation state treatment) not specified in provided text.
Dopant level fixed at 10%; optimal doping concentration per dopant not systematically varied.
Limited to Na0.66MnO2 host framework; generalizability to other Mn-free or multi-TM hosts not demonstrated.
Long-term cycling stability (>100 cycles) and full-cell performance not reported.
No direct quantification of oxygen redox capacity contribution vs. TM redox (e.g., via differential capacity or titration).
Compositional/structural factors favouring reversible oxygen redox are not fully understood, and predictive design strategies remain elusive.
Atomic-level origin of phase-dependent reversibility (P2 vs. P'2) in oxygen redox was unclear prior to this work.
No established descriptor for rational selection of dopants to stabilize anionic redox.

datasets_or_materials: Doped variants: K-, Cu-, Rb-, Cs-, Tl-doped Na0.66MnO2 (10% doping level)
P2 and P'2 polymorphs of each doped composition

important_claims.claim: Larger bond valence mismatch induces local structural distortions that destabilize non-bonding O-2p orbitals, enabling oxygen redox at ~4.2 V and ~4.5 V vs. Na/Na+.
important_claims.evidence: High-throughput screening revealed correlation between bond valence mismatch and oxygen activity; electrochemical testing confirmed high-voltage redox features in high-mismatch dopants.

important_claims.claim: P2 polymorph supports reversible oxygen redox, whereas P'2 leads to irreversible oxygen redox in otherwise identical doped compositions.
important_claims.evidence: Electrochemical testing showed stark phase-dependent reversibility; RIXS and XAS confirmed oxygen-centered redox only in P2.

important_claims.claim: Large dopants increase O–O separation, suppressing (O-O)n- dimer formation and O2 evolution.
important_claims.evidence: WT-EXAFS and structural analysis indicated dopant-induced expansion mitigating oxygen–oxygen interaction.

important_claims.claim: Cu doping stabilizes Mn lattice position by regulating metal migration, preventing irreversible structural reordering.
important_claims.evidence: WT-EXAFS showed suppressed Mn migration and Cu-mediated Mn site retention in P2.

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: Provides a transferable descriptor (bond valence mismatch) and phase-aware design rules for anionic redox cathodes; experimental protocols (synthesis of P2/P'2 polymorphs, RIXS/XAS/WT-EXAFS suite) are directly reusable for mechanistic studies of oxygen redox in other alkali-ion systems.

keynotes.title: Chemically Stabilized Nasicon-Na1.5V0.5Nb1.5(PO4)3 Anode for Long-Life Sodium-Ion Batteries

keynote.title: Chemically Stabilized Nasicon-Na1.5V0.5Nb1.5(PO4)3 Anode for Long-Life Sodium-Ion Batteries
keynote.core_problem: Structural degradation and rapid capacity decay in NASICON-type Nb2(PO4)3 anodes for sodium-ion batteries, caused by insufficient Na+ occupancy at the Na(1) site leading to lantern unit instability during cycling.
contributions: Introduction of V3+ substitution to achieve Na1.5V0.5Nb1.5(PO4)3, chemically stabilizing the NASICON framework by filling the Na(1) site
Demonstration of solid-solution (de)intercalation behavior instead of multi-phase reactions, enhancing structural reversibility
Achievement of high capacity retention (89% after 500 cycles at 5C) and exceptional rate capability (105 mAh g−1 at 5C)
Construction and validation of a full-cell configuration with NASICON-Na3V2(PO4)3 cathode delivering 98 Wh kg−1 and 80% capacity retention after 1000 cycles at 5C
methods: V3+ substitution into Nb2(PO4)3 to yield Na1.5V0.5Nb1.5(PO4)3
Density functional theory (DFT) calculations to predict redox activity and Na(1) site role
X-ray absorption spectroscopy (XAS) to confirm V3+/V2+ and Nb5+/Nb4+/Nb3+ redox couples
In-situ X-ray diffraction (XRD) to probe (de)intercalation mechanism
Galvanostatic intermittent titration technique (GITT) to estimate sodium diffusivity
Electrochemical testing: galvanostatic cycling, rate capability, full-cell assembly and evaluation
experiments_or_evidence: 39% capacity retention after 200 cycles for Nb2(PO4)3 anode
89% capacity retention after 500 cycles at 5C for Na1.5V0.5Nb1.5(PO4)3 anode
105 mAh g−1 capacity retained at 5C rate
Sodium diffusivity ≈ 10−9 cm² s−1 deduced from GITT and in-situ XRD
Solid-solution (de)intercalation confirmed via in-situ XRD (contrasted with two-phase behavior in Nb2(PO4)3)
Full-cell energy density of 98 Wh kg−1 (anode + cathode mass basis) and 80% capacity retention after 1000 cycles at 5C
assumptions: Na(1) site occupancy is critical for mechanical integrity of lantern units in NASICON framework
V3+ substitution enables both structural stabilization and additional redox activity without phase segregation
Solid-solution behavior inherently improves kinetic and cyclability performance compared to multi-phase reactions
limitations: No explicit discussion of scalability, synthesis cost, or air/moisture sensitivity of the material
No data on voltage hysteresis, Coulombic efficiency evolution, or low-temperature performance
Full-cell metrics exclude inactive components (current collectors, electrolyte, separator), limiting practical energy density assessment

Nb2(PO4)3 exhibits multiple two-phase reactions, whereas Na1.5V0.5Nb1.5(PO4)3 shows complete solid-solution formation — highlighting the underappreciated role of Na(1) site occupancy in governing reaction pathway
NASICON compounds are 'largely ignored as anodes due to their low capacity and high voltage'
Nb2(PO4)3 suffers from rapid capacity decay (39% retention after 200 cycles) due to structural degradation triggered by lack of sufficient sodium ions at Na(1) site

important_claims.claim: Na1.5V0.5Nb1.5(PO4)3 delivers ~140 mAh g−1 at 1.2 V vs. Na+/Na0 through Nb5+/Nb4+/Nb3+ and V3+/V2+ redox activities
important_claims.evidence: Predicted by DFT calculations and confirmed by X-ray absorption spectroscopy (XAS)

important_claims.evidence: Electrochemical full-cell cycling data reported

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: Provides a generalizable chemical stabilization strategy—targeted cation substitution to fill critical alkali sites—for improving structural resilience in polyanionic electrode frameworks; mechanistic insights on site-specific roles in NASICON (de)intercalation are transferable to other M2(PO4)3 systems.

keynotes.title: Suppression of Adverse Phase Transition of Layered Oxide Cathode via Local Electronic Structure Regulation for High-Capacity Sodium-Ion Batteries

keynote.title: Suppression of Adverse Phase Transition of Layered Oxide Cathode via Local Electronic Structure Regulation for High-Capacity Sodium-Ion Batteries
keynote.core_problem: Intricate phase transitions and structural degradation in O3-type layered oxide cathodes at high voltages (>4.0 V vs Na+/Na), causing rapid capacity decay.
contributions: Introduces a Li/Ti cosubstitution strategy to regulate local electronic structure of oxygen
Mitigates adverse phase transitions and strengthens electrostatic bonding within the transition metal (TM) layer
Induces and optimizes the O3-OP2 phase transition in the 2.0–4.3 V voltage window
Demonstrates improved high-voltage stability and cycling performance in sodium-ion battery cathodes
methods: Li/Ti cosubstitution into O3-type layered oxide cathode (NaLi1/9Ni1/3Mn4/9Ti1/9O2)
Electronic structure modulation targeting oxygen configuration
Electrochemical evaluation (capacity, cycling stability) at 1C in 2.0–4.3 V range
experiments_or_evidence: Achieved 161.2 mAh g⁻¹ capacity at 1C in 2.0–4.3 V
Stable cycling up to 100 cycles demonstrated
assumptions: Local electronic structure regulation of oxygen influences interlayer electrostatic forces
Strengthening TM-layer electrostatic bonding suppresses detrimental phase transitions
O3-OP2 phase transition is controllable and beneficial when optimized

gap_signals.text: O3-type cathodes suffer from intricate phase transition and structural degradation at high voltages (>4.0 V vs Na+/Na), resulting in rapid capacity decay.

important_claims.claim: Li/Ti cosubstitution mitigates phase transitions and counteracts weakening of the shielding effect upon Na⁺ extraction.
important_claims.evidence: Described as 'deliberate modulation' that simultaneously mitigates phase transitions and enhances electrostatic bonding within the TM layer; enables stable O3-OP2 transition in 2.0–4.3 V.

important_claims.claim: The cosubstituted cathode exhibits high capacity and stable cycling.
important_claims.evidence: 161.2 mAh g⁻¹ at 1C and stable cycling up to 100 cycles in 2.0–4.3 V.

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: Provides a mechanistic reference for element substitution strategies targeting oxygen electronic structure to control phase behavior and interlayer forces in O3-type layered cathodes; methodology potentially transferable to other alkali-ion systems.

keynotes.title: New Insight into Bulk Structural Degradation of High-Voltage LiCoO2 at 4.55 V

keynote.title: New Insight into Bulk Structural Degradation of High-Voltage LiCoO2 at 4.55 V
keynote.core_problem: Underlying mechanical and structural degradation mechanisms of high-voltage layered oxide cathodes (specifically LiCoO2) during charging to 4.55 V, leading to fast capacity fading.
contributions: Identification of kink band formation as a new type of mechanical degradation in Mg/Ti co-doped LiCoO2 at 4.55 V
Demonstration that kink bands accommodate local stress and impede crack propagation, thereby improving structural integrity in highly delithiated states
Discovery of coupled O3-to-O1 phase transformation accompanying kink band formation
Integration of machine-learning-aided atomic-resolution imaging and first-principles calculations to link mechanical deformation with structural transition
methods: Machine-learning-aided atomic-resolution imaging (for detecting kink bands and phase transformation)
First-principles calculations (to assess energetic favorability of O3-to-O1 transition)
Electrochemical charging to 4.55 V vs Li/Li+ (on Mg/Ti co-doped LiCoO2 cathode)
experiments_or_evidence: Observation of kink bands in Mg/Ti co-doped LiCoO2 after charging to 4.55 V
Correlative evidence linking kink band formation with suppressed crack propagation
Imaging-confirmed co-occurrence of kink bands and O3-to-O1 phase transformation
First-principles results showing energetic favorability of O1 phase under delithiated conditions
assumptions: Kink band formation is a stress-relief mechanism intrinsic to the crystallographic response of layered oxide under high-voltage delithiation
The observed O3-to-O1 transition is causally linked to mechanical strain induced by kink band formation, not merely electrochemical conditions alone
limitations: Findings specific to Mg/Ti co-doped LiCoO2; generalizability to other dopants or layered oxides (e.g., NMC, NCA) not established
No quantitative metrics on capacity retention improvement attributable solely to kink bands
Lack of in operando validation—observations are post-mortem

gap_signals.text: Findings specific to Mg/Ti co-doped LiCoO2; generalizability to other dopants or layered oxides (e.g., NMC, NCA) not established
Lack of in operando validation—observations are post-mortem
No quantitative metrics on capacity retention improvement attributable solely to kink bands
Coupling between electrochemically triggered mechanical failures and structural transition
Underlying degradation mechanisms remain elusive

datasets_or_materials: Mg and Ti co-doped LiCoO2 cathode material
Electrode samples charged to 4.55 V vs Li/Li+

important_claims.claim: Kink band formation accommodates local stress and impedes crack propagation, improving structural integrity in highly delithiated states.
important_claims.evidence: Local stress accommodation by kink bands observed; correlated with improved structural integrity in highly delithiated state.

important_claims.claim: Kink band formation is often accompanied by O3-to-O1 phase transformation.
important_claims.evidence: Machine-learning-aided atomic-resolution imaging revealed co-occurrence; supported by first-principles calculations showing energetic favorability.

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: Provides a microstructural design principle—intentional promotion of controlled kink band formation—as a strategy to enhance mechanical resilience in high-voltage layered oxides; methodological integration of ML-enhanced imaging with DFT offers a template for coupling nanoscale mechanics and phase thermodynamics in battery materials.

keynotes.title: Trimethyl Borate as Film-Forming Electrolyte Additive To Improve High-Voltage Performances

keynote.title: Trimethyl Borate as Film-Forming Electrolyte Additive To Improve High-Voltage Performances
keynote.core_problem: Electrolyte decomposition and cathode structural degradation at high voltages (e.g., 4.5 V) in LiCoO2-based Li-ion batteries.
contributions: Introduction of trimethyl borate (TMB) as a film-forming electrolyte additive for high-voltage LiCoO2 cells
Demonstration that TMB lowers oxidation potential of baseline electrolyte (from 4.25 V to 3.75 V vs. Li/Li+)
Evidence of formation of a ~25 nm compact protective cathode-electrolyte interphase (CEI) layer via preferential oxidation of TMB
Improved capacity retention (64% → 81% after 100 cycles at 0.1 C, 2.5–4.5 V) with 2.0 wt% TMB
methods: Electrochemical cycling (galvanostatic charge/discharge)
X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) to analyze surface chemistry and oxidation state
X-ray diffraction (XRD) to assess bulk structural integrity of LiCoO2
Raman spectroscopy to probe local structural changes in LiCoO2
experiments_or_evidence: Capacity retention comparison: 64% vs. 81% after 100 cycles (2.5–4.5 V, 0.1 C) with/without 2.0 wt% TMB
XPS showing oxidation products of TMB on cathode surface and suppressed electrolyte decomposition
XRD and Raman confirming better structural maintenance of LiCoO2 in TMB-containing electrolyte
Oxidation potential shift measured (baseline: 4.25 V → with TMB: 3.75 V vs. Li/Li+)
assumptions: Preferential oxidation of TMB drives protective CEI formation
The ~25 nm CEI layer is chemically stable and electronically insulating but ionically conductive
Observed electrochemical and spectroscopic improvements are causally linked to TMB-derived CEI
limitations: Limited to LiCoO2 cathode and specific voltage window (2.5–4.5 V)
No data on rate capability, long-term cycling beyond 100 cycles, or elevated temperature performance
No investigation of anode side effects (e.g., SEI modification, Li metal compatibility, or graphite stability)
No quantification of gas evolution or safety-related metrics (e.g., thermal runaway onset)

gap_signals.text: Limited to LiCoO2 cathode and specific voltage window (2.5–4.5 V)
No data on rate capability, long-term cycling beyond 100 cycles, or elevated temperature performance
No investigation of anode side effects (e.g., SEI modification, Li metal compatibility, or graphite stability)
No quantification of gas evolution or safety-related metrics (e.g., thermal runaway onset)
Results reported only for LiCoO2; no validation on Ni-rich or high-voltage spinel cathodes (e.g., LNMO).
Lack of direct chemical identification of CEI composition (e.g., B-O, B-F, or borate polymer species) beyond XPS elemental states.
No control experiments isolating TMB’s effect from other electrolyte components or comparing against other borate additives.

important_claims.claim: TMB lowers oxidation potential of baseline electrolyte from 4.25 V to 3.75 V vs. Li/Li+.
important_claims.evidence: Reported electrochemical measurement in text.

important_claims.claim: TMB forms a ~25 nm compact protection layer on the cathode surface via preferential oxidation.
important_claims.evidence: Stated directly; supported by XPS and electrochemical performance.

important_claims.claim: TMB addition improves capacity retention from 64% to 81% after 100 cycles (2.5–4.5 V, 0.1 C).
important_claims.evidence: Quantitative cycling result explicitly provided.

important_claims.claim: TMB suppresses electrolyte decomposition and protects LiCoO2 against structural degradation.
important_claims.evidence: XPS shows TMB oxidation and reduced decomposition signatures; XRD and Raman show improved structural retention.

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: High — provides a validated, low-cost borate-based additive design principle and experimental protocol (electrochemistry + XPS/XRD/Raman) applicable to screening CEI-forming additives for high-voltage cathodes.

keynotes.title: Complex‐Concentrated Anion Doping Enables Ultra‐Stable Lattice Oxygen and Structural Integrity in Lithium‐Rich Layered Oxide Cathodes

keynote.title: Complex‐Concentrated Anion Doping Enables Ultra‐Stable Lattice Oxygen and Structural Integrity in Lithium‐Rich Layered Oxide Cathodes
keynote.core_problem: Intrinsic oxygen instability and structural degradation in lithium- and manganese-rich (LMR) layered oxide cathodes, causing voltage fade and capacity decay.
contributions: Introduction of a complex-concentrated anion-doping paradigm (F, Br, S) into the oxygen sublattice of LMR cathodes
Achievement of ultra-stable local oxygen coordination environments during long-term cycling
Preservation of LiTM6 transition metal honeycomb ordering after electrochemical cycling
Realization of near-zero strain behavior (0.63% volume change at 4.8 V vs. Li+/Li), the first zero-strain LMR cathode
Demonstration of ultralow voltage fade (1 mV/cycle for first 100 cycles; negligible thereafter) and 93% energy retention after 200 cycles in pouch cells
methods: Complex-concentrated anion doping with F, Br, and S into the oxygen sublattice
X-ray absorption spectroscopy (XAS)
Aberration-corrected scanning transmission electron microscopy (AC-STEM)
experiments_or_evidence: XAS and AC-STEM confirmation of ultra-stable local oxygen coordination environments during long-term cycling
Suppression of detrimental phase transformations and oxygen-loss-induced cavitation
Preservation of LiTM6 honeycomb ordering post-cycling
Volume change measurement of 0.63% upon charging to 4.8 V vs. Li+/Li
Voltage fade quantification: 1 mV per cycle over first 100 cycles, then negligible
Energy retention of 93% after 200 cycles in pouch cell configuration

gap_signals.text: Lack of approaches targeting oxygen sublattice engineering via multi-anion doping to modulate chemo-mechanical coupling
LMR cathodes' full potential is hindered by intrinsic oxygen instability and structural degradation
Prior strategies fail to simultaneously stabilize lattice oxygen redox and preserve structural integrity at scale

datasets_or_materials: Lithium- and manganese-rich (LMR) layered oxide cathode material

important_claims.claim: Complex-concentrated anion doping (F, Br, S) enhances oxygen-redox and structural stability.
important_claims.evidence: XAS and AC-STEM confirm ultra-stable local oxygen coordination environments and inhibition of phase transformations and oxygen-loss-induced cavitation.

important_claims.claim: The LiTM6 transition metal honeycomb ordering is preserved after electrochemical cycling.
important_claims.evidence: Direct observation via aberration-corrected scanning transmission electron microscopy.

important_claims.claim: The doped LMR cathode exhibits near-zero strain behavior.
important_claims.evidence: Only 0.63% volume change measured upon charging to 4.8 V vs. Li+/Li.

important_claims.claim: The cathode delivers ultralow voltage fade and high energy retention in pouch cells.
important_claims.evidence: 1 mV/cycle voltage fade for first 100 cycles; 93% energy retention after 200 cycles in pouch cell configuration.

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: Provides a generalizable oxygen-sublattice engineering strategy for mitigating chemo-mechanical failure in ceramic intercalation electrodes beyond LMR systems.

keynotes.title: Monitoring the Species Formed at High Cathode Potentials at Ni-Rich Cathodes in Lithium-Ion Batteries Using a Novel Dual Working Electrode

keynote.title: Monitoring the Species Formed at High Cathode Potentials at Ni-Rich Cathodes in Lithium-Ion Batteries Using a Novel Dual Working Electrode
keynote.core_problem: Understanding parasitic reactions (e.g., O2 release, TM dissolution, HF/H+ formation) occurring at high voltages (>4.7 V vs. Li+/Li) in Ni-rich cathodes (e.g., NCA), which cause structural degradation and capacity fading in lithium-ion batteries.
contributions: Development of a simplified three-electrode dual-working-electrode cell setup using a carbon sense electrode to qualitatively detect redox-active parasitic species formed during high-voltage charging of Ni-rich cathodes.
Application of the setup to probe O2, H2O, H+, HF, and dissolved transition metal species (TMz+) across temperatures (10, 25, 45°C) via reductive currents at the sense electrode.
Cross-validation of reductive current signals with OEMS (gassing) and operando XAS (TM dissolution) data.
methods: Three-electrode electrochemical cell with LiNi0.80Co0.15Al0.05O2 (NCA) as working electrode (WE), Vulcan XC-72 carbon black as sense electrode (SE), and delithiated LiFePO4 (LFP) as counter electrode (CE).
Intermittent cyclic voltammetry (CV) charging protocol to 5.0 V vs. Li+/Li in LP47 electrolyte (1 M LiPF6 in EC:DEC, 3:7).
Holding SE at fixed potentials to selectively reduce target species; measuring resulting reductive currents as proxies for species formation.
Cross-correlation of SE reductive currents with OEMS-derived gassing traces and operando XAS-derived TM dissolution behavior.
experiments_or_evidence: Reductive current measurements at carbon SE during NCA WE charging to 5.0 V vs. Li+/Li at 10, 25, and 45°C.
Comparison of temperature-dependent reductive current profiles with OEMS-detected gas evolution (e.g., O2).
Comparison with operando XAS-measured transition metal dissolution kinetics.
assumptions: Target species (O2, H2O, H+, HF, TMz+) undergo electrochemical reduction at the carbon sense electrode at predictable, distinct potentials.
Reductive currents at the SE are solely attributable to the targeted parasitic species and not overlapping or side reactions.
The LFP CE maintains a stable, constant potential (~3.43 V vs. Li+/Li) throughout cycling due to capacitive oversizing and 90% delithiation.
limitations: Method provides qualitative, not quantitative, speciation.
Relies on assumed reduction potentials — no direct spectroscopic identification of species at SE.
Carbon electrode surface chemistry (e.g., functional groups, oxidation state) may influence reduction kinetics and selectivity, but is not characterized or controlled.
No validation of SE selectivity via control experiments (e.g., spiking known species, inert SE baseline).
Limited to species reducible at carbon within accessible potential window; non-reducible or passivating species remain undetected.

gap_signals.text: Limited to species reducible at carbon within accessible potential window; non-reducible or passivating species remain undetected.
Method provides qualitative, not quantitative, speciation.
No validation of SE selectivity via control experiments (e.g., spiking known species, inert SE baseline).
Relies on assumed reduction potentials — no direct spectroscopic identification of species at SE.
Carbon electrode surface chemistry (e.g., functional groups, oxidation state) may influence reduction kinetics and selectivity, but is not characterized or controlled.
Reliance on assumed reduction potentials at carbon — lacks in situ verification of species identity (e.g., no coupled spectroscopy at SE).
Explicitly stated: 'method provides qualitative rather than quantitative speciation'.
No reported control experiments isolating individual species or confirming SE response specificity.

datasets_or_materials: LiNi0.80Co0.15Al0.05O2 (NCA) cathode material (BASF TODA Battery Materials LLC, Japan)
Delithiated LiFePO4 (LFP) counter electrode (90% delithiated, capacitively oversized)
LP47 electrolyte (1 M LiPF6 in EC:DEC, 3:7)

important_claims.claim: Reductive currents at the carbon sense electrode correlate with formation of O2, H2O, H+, HF, and TMz+ during high-voltage NCA charging.
important_claims.evidence: Correlation of SE reductive current profiles with OEMS gassing traces and operando XAS TM dissolution behavior across 10–45°C.

important_claims.claim: The dual-working-electrode setup enables mechanistic probing of parasitic reactions without complex auxiliary instrumentation.
important_claims.evidence: Proof-of-concept use of carbon SE to quantify oxygen release via reductive current; extension here to multiple species and temperatures.

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: High — offers a low-cost, accessible electrochemical screening platform for detecting redox-active degradation species in battery cathodes; adaptable to other CAMs or electrolytes, though requires careful calibration and validation of SE selectivity.

keynotes.title: Multifunctional role of gallium-doping in O3-type layered-oxide cathodes for sodium-ion batteries: Enhancing bulk-to-surface stability.

keynote.title: Multifunctional role of gallium-doping in O3-type layered-oxide cathodes for sodium-ion batteries: Enhancing bulk-to-surface stability
keynote.core_problem: Irreversible oxygen redox reaction at high cutoff voltage (4.3 V vs. Na+/Na) causes accelerated capacity degradation in O3-type layered-oxide cathodes for sodium-ion batteries.
contributions: Demonstrates multifunctional role of Ga³⁺ doping in enhancing structural ordering, widening Na⁺ transport pathways, and improving bulk/interface stability.
Reveals Ga³⁺-induced modulation of O 2p states near Fermi level, suppressing lattice oxygen redox, oxygen vacancy formation, and electrolyte decomposition.
Provides experimental and DFT evidence linking Ga³⁺ doping to improved structural reversibility, mechanical stability, capacity retention, and rate capability.
methods: Synthesis of Ga³⁺-doped O3-type Na₀.₉Zn₀.₀₇Ni₀.₃₈₋₀.₅ₓGaₓMn₀.₄₅₋₀.₅ₓTi₀.₁O₂ cathode materials.
Systematic characterization of bulk/interface properties (structural ordering, Na⁺ transport barrier, mechanical stability).
Electrochemical testing (cycling at 100 mA g⁻¹, rate capability up to 2000 mA g⁻¹, voltage range 2.2–4.3 V).
Density functional theory (DFT) calculations to analyze electronic structure (O 2p state modulation, charge compensation mechanisms).
experiments_or_evidence: Higher capacity retention after 100 cycles at 100 mA g⁻¹: 86.4% (Ga-doped) vs. 68.1% (pristine).
Better rate capability at 2000 mA g⁻¹: 94.1 mAh g⁻¹ (Ga-doped) vs. 80.0 mAh g⁻¹ (pristine).
DFT calculations showing Ga³⁺ modulates O 2p states near Fermi level, mitigating oxygen redox and related side reactions.

gap_signals.text: Irreversible oxygen redox reaction at high voltages often leads accelerated capacity degradation.

important_claims.claim: Ga³⁺ incorporation enhances structural ordering and widens Na⁺ transport pathways, reducing Na⁺ transport barrier.
important_claims.evidence: Systematic examination of bulk/interface properties; improved electrochemical performance (rate capability, cycling stability).

important_claims.claim: Ga³⁺ doping modulates the O 2p state near the Fermi level, mitigating oxygen redox, oxygen vacancy formation, and electrolyte decomposition.
important_claims.evidence: Density functional theory calculations.

important_claims.claim: Ga³⁺-doped material demonstrates superior structural reversibility and mechanical stability during cycling.
important_claims.evidence: Comparative electrochemical and structural analysis versus pristine material.

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: Provides a design principle—targeted cation doping to suppress oxygen redox via electronic structure modulation—for stabilizing high-voltage O3-type cathodes in SIBs; methodology (combined synthesis, electrochemistry, DFT) is transferable to other dopant or layered oxide systems.

evidence_type_annotations.claim: CTAB-assisted co-precipitation

Uniform internal doping of Zr⁴⁺ into primary particles of LiNi₀.₈₉Mn₀.₁Zr₀.₀₁O₂ via CTAB-assisted co-precipitation suppresses lattice strain, enhances structural stability, and prolongs cycling life without cobalt.
Conventional solid-state or post-synthesis doping techniques suffer from uneven dopant distribution and dopant segregation at grain boundaries.

The elimination of Co often results in increased Li/Ni cation disorder, reduced electronic conductivity, and a tendency for anisotropic lattice distortion, particularly during the H2–H3 phase transition in the highly delithiated state.

evidence_type_annotations.claim: first-principles calculations

Establishes a theoretical foundation for stability and electrochemical activity of eREAL materials; identifies F substitution as a viable route to extend reversible voltage window; reveals trade-off between elevated cation redox potentials and Cl oxidation/Cl-Cl dimerization.

Cl oxidation and Cl-Cl dimerization at high voltages may limit stability; flat voltage profiles pose electrochemical compatibility challenges when paired with active materials operating at different or wider voltage ranges.
Full text excerpt:
As a redox-active catholyte, they could significantly increase the energy density of solid-state batteries.
We map the phase stability of eREAL structures with varying metal-to-Cl ratio, transition-metal species, oxidation states, and anion frameworks, and compute cation and anion redox potentials.

We find that the high ionicity of metal-Cl bonds elevates cation redox potentials above those of conventional oxide cathodes, but also will promote Cl oxidation and Cl-Cl dimerization at high voltages, which may limit the stability of these materials.

Reveals CEI formation and dissolution mechanisms including a two-step LiF dissolution pathway and potential-dependent CEI morphology/composition
Findings require validation across different potential windows and with non-carbonate electrolytes
Full text excerpt:

When cycled between 2.5 and 5.5 V, an anodic current peak indicates the formation of a 36 nm amorphous thin film without crystalline LiF, attributed to EC oxidation producing HF and subsequent LiF at a higher potential.

These results provide new insights into CEI formation and dissolution mechanisms, underscoring the need for further studies across different potential windows and with non carbonate electrolytes to validate these findings.

evidence_type_annotations.claim: electrolyte additive formulation with trimethyl borate (TMB)
TMB forms a ~25 nm compact cathode-electrolyte interphase layer via preferential oxidation, suppressing electrolyte decomposition and structural degradation of LiCoO2 at 4.5 V.
The capacity retention of the cell after 100 cycles between 2.5 and 4.5 V at 0.1 C increases from 64 to 81% when 2.0 wt % TMB is added into the baseline electrolyte.
Full text excerpt:
The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopic results demonstrate the oxidation of TMB on the cathode and therefore the suppressed decomposition of the electrolyte.
The results of the X-ray diffraction and Raman spectroscopy show the better structural maintenance of the LiCoO2 material in the TMB-containing electrolyte.

evidence_type_annotations.claim: complex-concentrated anion doping (F, Br, S) into oxygen sublattice

Achieves ultra-stable lattice oxygen and structural integrity in lithium-rich layered oxide cathodes via complex-concentrated anion doping, enabling the first zero-strain LMR cathode with ultralow voltage fade and high energy retention.
Here, we present a complex‐concentrated anion‐doping paradigm in which multiple anions, F, Br, and S, are incorporated into the oxygen sublattice to enhance oxygen‐redox and structural stability.
Full text excerpt:
However, their full potential is hindered by intrinsic oxygen instability and structural degradation, resulting in pronounced voltage fade and capacity decay.

The resulting LMR cathode delivers ultralow voltage fade (1 mV per cycle during the first 100 cycles and becomes negligible in subsequent cycles) and outstanding energy retention (93% after 200 cycles) in a pouch cell configuration.

evidence_type_annotations.claim: dual working electrode electrochemical setup

A simplified dual working electrode cell design enabling qualitative analysis of parasitic species (O2, H2O, H+, HF, TMz+) formed during high-voltage charging of Ni-rich cathodes without requiring complex auxiliary instrumentation.
The method provides qualitative rather than quantitative speciation, and relies on assumed reduction potentials of target species at the carbon sense electrode.

references: Weiliang Li et al. (2025) Multifunctional role of gallium-doping in O3-type layered-oxide cathodes for sodium-ion batteries: Enhancing bulk-to-surface stability. doi:10.1016/j.jcis.2025.137484

evidence_type_annotations.claim: doped electrocatalyst

Ga3+ doping enhances bulk-to-surface stability in O3-type layered-oxide cathodes by improving structural ordering, widening Na+ transport pathways, modulating O 2p states, and suppressing oxygen redox side reactions at high voltage.
However, the irreversible oxygen redox reaction at high voltages often leads accelerated capacity degradation.
Full text excerpt:
Ga3+ incorporation enhances the structural ordering of the layered framework and widens Na+ transport pathways, thereby reducing Na+ transport barrier.

As evidenced by the density functional theory calculations, Ga3+ doping modulates the O 2p state near the Fermi level, mitigating the charge compensation mechanism of lattice oxygen, oxygen vacancy formation, and electrolyte decomposition at high voltages.

evidence_type_annotations.claim: oxygen vacancy migration analysis

Identification of a distinct oxygen release mechanism in LiCoO2 driven by oxygen vacancy-facilitated cation migration and reduction, preserving structural integrity in defect-free regions while causing surface degradation.

evidence_type_annotations.claim: CTAB-assisted co-precipitation

Uniform internal doping of Zr⁴⁺ into primary particles of LiNi₀.₈₉Mn₀.₁Zr₀.₀₁O₂ via CTAB-assisted co-precipitation suppresses lattice strain, enhances structural stability, and prolongs cycling life without cobalt.
Conventional solid-state or post-synthesis doping techniques suffer from uneven dopant distribution and dopant segregation at grain boundaries.

evidence_type_annotations.claim: electrolyte additive formulation with trimethyl borate (TMB)
TMB forms a ~25 nm compact cathode-electrolyte interphase layer via preferential oxidation, suppressing electrolyte decomposition and structural degradation of LiCoO2 at 4.5 V.

evidence_type_annotations.claim: complex-concentrated anion doping (F, Br, S) into oxygen sublattice

Achieves ultra-stable lattice oxygen and structural integrity in lithium-rich layered oxide cathodes via complex-concentrated anion doping, enabling the first zero-strain LMR cathode with ultralow voltage fade and high energy retention.
Here, we present a complex‐concentrated anion‐doping paradigm in which multiple anions, F, Br, and S, are incorporated into the oxygen sublattice to enhance oxygen‐redox and structural stability.

evidence_type_annotations.claim: oxygen vacancy migration analysis

Identification of a distinct oxygen release mechanism in LiCoO2 driven by oxygen vacancy-facilitated cation migration and reduction, preserving structural integrity in defect-free regions while causing surface degradation.

Full text excerpt: Identification of a distinct oxygen release mechanism in LiCoO2 driven by oxygen vacancy-facilitated cation migration and reduction, preserving structural integrity in defect-free regions while causing surface degradation.
Full text excerpt:
This work unveils the thermal vulnerability of high-voltage Li-ion batteries and the critical role of the surface fraction as a general mitigating approach.

references: Jiwoong Oh et al. (2025) Surfactant-Assisted Bulk Doping of High-Valence Ions for Structurally Stable Co-Free High-Ni Cathodes doi:10.1149/ma2025-0283396mtgabs

evidence_type_annotations.claim: CTAB-assisted co-precipitation

Uniform internal doping of Zr⁴⁺ into primary particles of LiNi₀.₈₉Mn₀.₁Zr₀.₀₁O₂ via CTAB-assisted co-precipitation suppresses lattice strain, enhances structural stability, and prolongs cycling life without cobalt.
Conventional solid-state or post-synthesis doping techniques suffer from uneven dopant distribution and dopant segregation at grain boundaries.
Full text excerpt:

The elimination of Co often results in increased Li/Ni cation disorder, reduced electronic conductivity, and a tendency for anisotropic lattice distortion, particularly during the H2–H3 phase transition in the highly delithiated state.

method_scenario_benchmark_triples.scenario: Li-M-Cl (M = 3d transition metals) ternary cathode materials
method_scenario_benchmark_triples.benchmark: cation and anion redox potentials | phase stability | voltage profile flatness
references: Zhuohan Li et al. (2026) Redox-Active Halide Materials for Cathode Applications arXiv:2606.07815v1

evidence_type_annotations.claim: first-principles calculations

Establishes a theoretical foundation for stability and electrochemical activity of eREAL materials; identifies F substitution as a viable route to extend reversible voltage window; reveals trade-off between elevated cation redox potentials and Cl oxidation/Cl-Cl dimerization.

Cl oxidation and Cl-Cl dimerization at high voltages may limit stability; flat voltage profiles pose electrochemical compatibility challenges when paired with active materials operating at different or wider voltage ranges.
Full text excerpt:
As a redox-active catholyte, they could significantly increase the energy density of solid-state batteries.
We map the phase stability of eREAL structures with varying metal-to-Cl ratio, transition-metal species, oxidation states, and anion frameworks, and compute cation and anion redox potentials.

We find that the high ionicity of metal-Cl bonds elevates cation redox potentials above those of conventional oxide cathodes, but also will promote Cl oxidation and Cl-Cl dimerization at high voltages, which may limit the stability of these materials.

method_scenario_benchmark_triples.scenario: cathode electrolyte interphase (CEI) formation in lithium-ion batteries
references: Kevyn Gallegos-Moncayo et al. (2025) Investigation of Cathode Electrolyte Interphase Formation via Coupling Liquid Electrochemical TEM and GC/MS arXiv:2510.12467v1

evidence_type_annotations.claim: microscopy imaging
Reveals CEI formation and dissolution mechanisms including a two-step LiF dissolution pathway and potential-dependent CEI morphology/composition
Findings require validation across different potential windows and with non-carbonate electrolytes
Full text excerpt:

When cycled between 2.5 and 5.5 V, an anodic current peak indicates the formation of a 36 nm amorphous thin film without crystalline LiF, attributed to EC oxidation producing HF and subsequent LiF at a higher potential.

These results provide new insights into CEI formation and dissolution mechanisms, underscoring the need for further studies across different potential windows and with non carbonate electrolytes to validate these findings.

Chemical stabilization of NASICON anode via V3+ substitution enabling Na(1) site filling, resulting in solid-solution Na (de)intercalation, enhanced sodium diffusivity, and long-cycle stability in full Na-ion cells.

evidence_type_annotations.claim: Li/Ti cosubstitution

Li/Ti cosubstitution suppresses adverse phase transition and structural degradation by regulating local electronic structure of oxygen, enhancing electrostatic bonding in TM layer and optimizing O3-OP2 phase transition.
However, this type of cathode often suffers from intricate phase transition and structural degradation at high voltages (i.e., >4.0 V vs Na+/Na), resulting in rapid capacity decay.

Identification of kink band formation as a new type of mechanical degradation that impedes crack propagation and couples with O3-to-O1 phase transition under high-voltage charging.

method_scenario_benchmark_triples.method: electrolyte additive formulation with trimethyl borate (TMB)
method_scenario_benchmark_triples.scenario: LiCoO2-based lithium-ion battery cathode at high voltage (4.5 V)
references: Qiuyan Liu et al. (2019) Trimethyl Borate as Film-Forming Electrolyte Additive To Improve High-Voltage Performances. doi:10.1021/acsami.9b03417

evidence_type_annotations.claim: electrolyte additive formulation with trimethyl borate (TMB)
TMB forms a ~25 nm compact cathode-electrolyte interphase layer via preferential oxidation, suppressing electrolyte decomposition and structural degradation of LiCoO2 at 4.5 V.
The capacity retention of the cell after 100 cycles between 2.5 and 4.5 V at 0.1 C increases from 64 to 81% when 2.0 wt % TMB is added into the baseline electrolyte.
Full text excerpt:
The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopic results demonstrate the oxidation of TMB on the cathode and therefore the suppressed decomposition of the electrolyte.
The results of the X-ray diffraction and Raman spectroscopy show the better structural maintenance of the LiCoO2 material in the TMB-containing electrolyte.

method_scenario_benchmark_triples.method: complex-concentrated anion doping (F, Br, S) into oxygen sublattice
references: Lei Wang et al. (2026) Complex‐Concentrated Anion Doping Enables Ultra‐Stable Lattice Oxygen and Structural Integrity in Lithium‐Rich Layered Oxide Cathodes doi:10.1002/adma.202522399

evidence_type_annotations.claim: complex-concentrated anion doping (F, Br, S) into oxygen sublattice

Achieves ultra-stable lattice oxygen and structural integrity in lithium-rich layered oxide cathodes via complex-concentrated anion doping, enabling the first zero-strain LMR cathode with ultralow voltage fade and high energy retention.
Here, we present a complex‐concentrated anion‐doping paradigm in which multiple anions, F, Br, and S, are incorporated into the oxygen sublattice to enhance oxygen‐redox and structural stability.
Full text excerpt:
However, their full potential is hindered by intrinsic oxygen instability and structural degradation, resulting in pronounced voltage fade and capacity decay.

The resulting LMR cathode delivers ultralow voltage fade (1 mV per cycle during the first 100 cycles and becomes negligible in subsequent cycles) and outstanding energy retention (93% after 200 cycles) in a pouch cell configuration.

evidence_type_annotations.claim: dual working electrode electrochemical setup

A simplified dual working electrode cell design enabling qualitative analysis of parasitic species (O2, H2O, H+, HF, TMz+) formed during high-voltage charging of Ni-rich cathodes without requiring complex auxiliary instrumentation.
The method provides qualitative rather than quantitative speciation, and relies on assumed reduction potentials of target species at the carbon sense electrode.

references: Weiliang Li et al. (2025) Multifunctional role of gallium-doping in O3-type layered-oxide cathodes for sodium-ion batteries: Enhancing bulk-to-surface stability. doi:10.1016/j.jcis.2025.137484

evidence_type_annotations.claim: doped electrocatalyst

Ga3+ doping enhances bulk-to-surface stability in O3-type layered-oxide cathodes by improving structural ordering, widening Na+ transport pathways, modulating O 2p states, and suppressing oxygen redox side reactions at high voltage.
However, the irreversible oxygen redox reaction at high voltages often leads accelerated capacity degradation.
Full text excerpt:
Ga3+ incorporation enhances the structural ordering of the layered framework and widens Na+ transport pathways, thereby reducing Na+ transport barrier.

As evidenced by the density functional theory calculations, Ga3+ doping modulates the O 2p state near the Fermi level, mitigating the charge compensation mechanism of lattice oxygen, oxygen vacancy formation, and electrolyte decomposition at high voltages.

references: Congli Sun et al. (2020) High Voltage Cycling Induced Thermal Vulnerability in LiCoO2 Cathode: Cation Loss and Oxygen Release Driven by Oxygen Vacancy Migration. doi:10.1021/acsnano.0c02237

evidence_type_annotations.claim: oxygen vacancy migration analysis

Identification of a distinct oxygen release mechanism in LiCoO2 driven by oxygen vacancy-facilitated cation migration and reduction, preserving structural integrity in defect-free regions while causing surface degradation.

Full text excerpt: Identification of a distinct oxygen release mechanism in LiCoO2 driven by oxygen vacancy-facilitated cation migration and reduction, preserving structural integrity in defect-free regions while causing surface degradation.

evidence_type_annotations.claim: CTAB-assisted co-precipitation

Uniform internal doping of Zr⁴⁺ into primary particles of LiNi₀.₈₉Mn₀.₁Zr₀.₀₁O₂ via CTAB-assisted co-precipitation suppresses lattice strain, enhances structural stability, and prolongs cycling life without cobalt.
Conventional solid-state or post-synthesis doping techniques suffer from uneven dopant distribution and dopant segregation at grain boundaries.

Establishes a theoretical foundation for stability and electrochemical activity of eREAL materials; identifies F substitution as a viable route to extend reversible voltage window; reveals trade-off between elevated cation redox potentials and Cl oxidation/Cl-Cl dimerization.

proponent_position.hypothesis: High-valence ion segregation at grain boundaries in Ni-rich cathodes arises not from thermodynamic equilibrium but from kinetic trapping during rapid calcination, and can be suppressed by controlled ramp-rate annealing combined with CTAB-mediated surface passivation. CTAB adsorption on primary particle surfaces reduces interfacial energy and slows grain boundary migration during calcination; simultaneous ramp-rate control prevents thermal runaway that drives high-valence ion diffusion to boundaries.
proponent_position.claimed_mechanism: CTAB adsorption on primary particle surfaces reduces interfacial energy and slows grain boundary migration during calcination; simultaneous ramp-rate control prevents thermal runaway that drives high-valence ion diffusion to boundaries. Compare three synthesis routes: (1) conventional fast-ramp calcination, (2) slow-ramp only, (3) CTAB-assisted + slow-ramp. Characterize via STEM-EDS line scans across grain boundaries, XRD Rietveld refinement for site occupancy, and electrochemical voltage decay profiling over 500 cycles.
causal_chain: CTAB adsorption on primary particle surfaces reduces interfacial energy and slows grain boundary migration during calcination; simultaneous ramp-rate control prevents thermal runaway that drives high-valence ion diffusion to boundaries.

proponent_response.revised_hypothesis: High-valence ion segregation at grain boundaries in Ni-rich cathodes arises not from thermodynamic equilibrium but from kinetic trapping during rapid calcination, and can be suppressed by controlled ramp-rate annealing combined with CTAB-mediated surface passivation. CTAB adsorption on primary particle surfaces reduces interfacial energy and slows grain boundary migration during calcination; simultaneous ramp-rate control prevents thermal runaway that drives high-valence ion diffusion to boundaries. Revision after Socratic Clarification: the hypothesis is limited to Co-free high-Ni cathodes; temperature is varied under matched baseline/control conditions; capacity (161.2 mAh g-1), cycling stability (100 cycles) must change in the predicted direction and the causal chain must survive source mapping, ablation, and regime-shift tests. Revision after Evidence and CAWM Layer 1-2: the hypothesis is limited to Co-free high-Ni cathodes; temperature is varied under matched baseline/control conditions; capacity (161.2 mAh g-1), cycling stability (100 cycles) must change in the predicted direction and the causal chain must survive source mapping, ablation, and regime-shift tests. Revision after Methodology and Regime Shift: the hypothesis is limited to Co-free high-Ni cathodes; temperature is varied under matched baseline/control conditions; capacity (161.2 mAh g-1), cycling stability (100 cycles) must change in the predicted direction and the causal chain must survive source mapping, ablation, and regime-shift tests.
proponent_response.revised_mechanism: CTAB adsorption on primary particle surfaces reduces interfacial energy and slows grain boundary migration during calcination; simultaneous ramp-rate control prevents thermal runaway that drives high-valence ion diffusion to boundaries. Compare three synthesis routes: (1) conventional fast-ramp calcination, (2) slow-ramp only, (3) CTAB-assisted + slow-ramp. Characterize via STEM-EDS line scans across grain boundaries, XRD Rietveld refinement for site occupancy, and electrochemical voltage decay profiling over 500 cycles. Revised mechanism: CTAB-assisted co-precipitation must produce an observable intermediate change in Co-free high-Ni cathodes; otherwise any endpoint change in capacity (161.2 mAh g-1), cycling stability (100 cycles) is treated as correlation rather than mechanism.
